---
title: "Privacy in pmxSynthData"
author: "Andrew Stein"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    toc: true
    number_sections: true
    fig_width: 10
    fig_height: 5.5
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Privacy in pmxSynthData}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>", fig.align = "center")
library(pmxSynthData)
```

# Purpose before mathematics

`pmxSynthData` makes source-calibrated **mock data for model-workflow
exploration**. It learns a few coarse population features and creates new PMX
event tables for joins, reshaping, plots, control-file plumbing, and early
model-code debugging. It does not fit a PK, PD, or NLME model. Its
deliberately limited fidelity is unsuitable for estimation, inference, model
selection, scientific conclusions, clinical decisions, or claims that a source
distribution was reproduced.

There are two sharply separated stages. `fit_private_pmx()` is the only
stage allowed to read confidential rows. `generate_pmx()` receives only the
released model. There are no raw patient anchors, nearest-neighbor donors,
copied source event skeletons, or exact-source-timing modes.

This vignette explains the privacy guarantee, its assumptions, and its
limitations. See `vignette("pmxSynthData-simulation-method")` for the actual
patient-simulation algorithm—including the fixed-grid trajectory model,
sampling-time probabilities, interpolation, and variability—and
`vignette("pmxSynthData-demo")` for runnable examples.

```{r pipeline, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="The Version 2 privacy boundary and post-processing pipeline."}
old <- par(mar = c(.5, .5, .5, .5), xpd = NA)
plot.new(); plot.window(xlim = c(0, 14), ylim = c(0, 7))
box <- function(x, y, label, fill, width = 2.7) {
  rect(x - width / 2, y - .55, x + width / 2, y + .55,
       col = fill, border = "#24445C", lwd = 1.5)
  text(x, y, label, cex = .76)
}
top_x <- c(1.7, 5.2, 8.7, 12.2)
top_lab <- c("Source + public\nconfiguration", "Bound complete\nsubjects",
             "Fixed-dimensional\nvectors", "OpenDP release\n+ accounting")
top_fill <- c("#FADBD8", rep("#D6EAF8", 2), "#FCF3CF")
for (i in seq_along(top_x)) box(top_x[i], 5.5, top_lab[i], top_fill[i])
for (i in seq_len(length(top_x) - 1L)) {
  arrows(top_x[i] + 1.38, 5.5, top_x[i + 1L] - 1.38, 5.5,
         length = .08, col = "#566573", lwd = 1.3)
}
abline(h = 3.65, lty = 2, col = "#7F8C8D", lwd = 1.5)
text(.3, 4.05, "restricted fitting", adj = 0, col = "#922B21")
text(.3, 3.25, "released model + post-processing", adj = 0,
     col = "#196F3D")
bottom_x <- c(11.7, 7.3, 2.9)
bottom_lab <- c("Private model\n+ ledger", "Generate new\nevent tables",
                "Validate, inspect,\nand export")
for (i in seq_along(bottom_x)) box(bottom_x[i], 1.6, bottom_lab[i], "#D5F5E3")
arrows(12.2, 4.94, 11.7, 2.17, length = .08, col = "#566573", lwd = 1.3)
arrows(10.32, 1.6, 8.68, 1.6, length = .08, col = "#566573", lwd = 1.3)
arrows(5.92, 1.6, 4.28, 1.6, length = .08, col = "#566573", lwd = 1.3)
par(old)
```

# Privacy for a pharmacometrician starting from zero

## Removing names is not sufficient

A longitudinal record can be recognizable through unusual measurements, a
rare infusion, an exceptional visit schedule, or a distinctive censoring
pattern. Removing a name blocks one direct route to identity; it does not stop
linkage to an outside clinic schedule, publication, or other auxiliary
information.

Revealing that an unusual record existed is not automatically the same as
identifying its owner. It can become identifying when combined with outside
information. Even without a name, a release may support an attribute inference
such as “the linked person probably had a very low biomarker value.”

| Concept | Question | Important distinction |
|---|---|---|
| Membership disclosure | Did this person's complete record influence the release? | Not proof of identity by itself |
| Attribute disclosure | Did an unusual response, schedule, or censoring pattern materially affect output? | Need not reveal a name |
| Linkage | Can released information be joined to auxiliary information? | Dropping ID does not prevent it |
| Re-identification | Can information be connected to a person? | DP does not promise impossibility |

## Two worlds and a one-person influence limit

Subject-level differential privacy compares dataset $D$, containing one
person's complete record, with neighboring dataset $D'$, which removes that
entire person. The output distributions must remain close even if an attacker
knows every other record and has auxiliary information.

Epsilon, $\epsilon$, is the **one-person influence limit**. At
$\epsilon=0$, output cannot depend on patient data at all. Smaller positive
epsilon permits less influence and is stronger; larger epsilon permits more
influence and is weaker. A mechanism can technically be DP with a very large
epsilon while giving weak practical protection. There is no universal safe
default.

```{r epsilon-table, echo=FALSE}
eps <- c(0, .1, .5, 1, 3, 10)
knitr::kable(data.frame(
  epsilon = eps, exp_epsilon = round(exp(eps), 4),
  interpretation = c("No source dependence", "Very small allowance",
                     "Smaller allowance", "Larger allowance",
                     "Potentially weak", "Very weak")
), caption = "Epsilon is an influence bound, not a risk percentage.")
```

Delta, $\delta$, is a very small additive allowance in the probability bound.
Smaller is stronger, and `delta = 0` is supported. Delta is **not** a
re-identification probability, the percentage of unprotected patients, or a
direct breach probability. Positive delta requires explicit justification.
The current Laplace releases realize pure DP (`delta = 0`), which also satisfies
any requested larger delta; the ledger records the realized value as zero.

For a numerical example, take $\epsilon=0.5$, $\delta=10^{-6}$, and an output
event whose probability without one person is 0.02. With that person, its
probability is bounded by

$$e^{0.5}(0.02)+10^{-6}\approx0.032975.$$

This is a bound on an arbitrary output event in two neighboring worlds, not a
claim that re-identification has probability 0.032975.

> Output generated entirely from a valid private model is formally subject-level `(epsilon, delta)`-differentially private. This means one person's complete record has a mathematically bounded influence on the release. It does not mean zero disclosure risk or guaranteed impossibility of re-identification.

## Is privacy protected?

Privacy is formally protected only when output is generated entirely from a
valid subject-level differentially private fitted model, with the reported
epsilon, delta, contribution bounds, public inputs, cumulative accounting, and
implementation assumptions. The protection is mathematically bounded rather
than absolute.

DP does not correct facts already known outside the release, secure a
compromised environment, prove that declared public inputs really were public,
or make a release legally anonymous. Privacy, legal, information-security,
ethics, and data-governance authorization remain separate reviews.

Small Phase 1 studies keep the mathematical guarantee, but utility is harder:
one of six people is a large part of the study. The package learns only broad
magnitude, coarse shape, generalized timing, and minimal variability rather
than weakening the privacy unit or silently increasing epsilon.

# Formal contract and privacy unit

For every neighboring $D,D'$ differing by add-or-remove of one complete
subject, every set of possible releases $S$, and randomized fitting mechanism
$M$,

$$\Pr[M(D)\in S]\le e^\epsilon\Pr[M(D')\in S]+\delta.$$

$\Pr$ is probability over private mechanism randomness; $M(D)$ is the released
model; $S$ is any collection of outputs; $\epsilon$ is the multiplicative
influence bound; and $\delta$ is the additive allowance. The subject privacy
unit includes every row, visit, actual and nominal time, dose, infusion,
occasion, covariate, endpoint value, DVID, missingness state, censoring state,
and protocol deviation. Row-level privacy is not substituted.

# Public configuration is part of the proof

Before confidential values are inspected, the caller declares roles and
exclusions; schema/classes/levels; endpoint DVID, units, clock and transform;
plausible numeric bounds; contribution limits; genuinely public protocol
values and grids; generator variability; and budget fractions.

`pmx_schema()` records metadata, but calling it asserts those metadata are
public. It is not a DP mechanism for discovering confidential columns or factor
levels. Exact source extrema are never automatic bounds. Date/POSIX and likely
direct-identifier columns stop fitting unless explicitly excluded.

| Information | Population example | One-person example | Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Protocol | Planned Q24H doses | One deviation | Public only if independently established; otherwise private summary |
| Endpoint | Broad rise then fall | One extreme peak | Bounded grid summary plus DP noise |
| Timing | Hourly versus weekly | One rare visit vector | Generalized cells; never copy a vector |
| Covariate | Coarse weight center | One weight | Bounded moment or public category |
| Identity | New practical IDs | Source ID | Source IDs never enter the fitted model |
| Linkage | Outside trial knowledge | Matching a rare schedule | DP limits added participation influence, not outside facts |

# Contribution bounds and fixed-dimensional subjects

The fitter deterministically truncates each subject and clips values to public
domains. Dose starts, occasions, observations per endpoint/occasion, timing
cells, and total rows are capped before **every** aggregate. Which people were
clipped is not released.

Each subject becomes a low-dimensional vector containing generalized regimen
features; timing-cell presence; endpoint-cell presence and bounded transformed
means; numeric covariate moments or public-category indicators; and coarse
censoring frequencies/limits. No vector contains an ID. No vector, raw row,
profile, event skeleton, or residual is serialized.

For a group with $p$ coordinates, let $\mathbf q_i\in[0,1]^p$ be subject $i$'s
bounded vector. The sum

$$\mathbf s(D)=\sum_{i\in D}\mathbf q_i$$

has L1 sensitivity at most $p$. With group budget $\epsilon_g$, OpenDP releases

$$\widetilde{\mathbf s}=\mathbf s(D)+\operatorname{Lap}_{p/\epsilon_g}.$$

A separate subject count has sensitivity one. Noisy means, proportions,
variances, smoothing, and constraint repair are post-processing. This
dimension-dependent sensitivity is conservative and can be noisy; shortening a
grid is often preferable to learning another fragile relationship from six
people.

# Six-patient worked privacy example

Suppose a public protocol declares two doses, four PK cells, four global PD
cells, weight bounds 40--120 kg, maximum 24 rows, and two occasions. Patient 6
has 31 rows, three dose starts, and nine PK observations in occasion 1. Before
aggregation, only the allowed 24 rows, two doses, and per-occasion observations
remain; weight is clipped and normalized. The model never reports that patient
6 was clipped.

For requested $\epsilon=2$, $\delta=0$, consider this illustrative allocation:

```{r six-budget, echo=FALSE}
b <- data.frame(
  group = c("count", "event/regimen", "timing", "covariates",
            "endpoint trajectories", "censoring"),
  fraction = c(.10, .15, .15, .10, .45, .05)
)
b$epsilon <- 2 * b$fraction
knitr::kable(b, caption = "Illustrative allocation, not a recommendation.")
```

If the endpoint vector has 16 coordinates, sensitivity is 16 and OpenDP scale
is $16/0.9\approx17.78$ in normalized-sum units. Removing patient 6 changes its
unnoised vector by at most 16 in L1 distance, so OpenDP's privacy map bounds that
query by 0.9 epsilon. Basic composition sums all query epsilons to 2. Generating
one or ten thousand datasets from this model stays at epsilon 2; a second fit
is a second release that must be composed organizationally.

The noise may exceed a six-subject sum. Public protocol structure and broad
public generator variability therefore carry much workflow utility without
spending budget on detailed fidelity.

# Backend and accounting

Production fitting uses the OpenDP R package's Laplace measurement over finite
64-bit floating-point values. The adapter constructs scalar/vector domains,
uses absolute or L1 metrics, checks the sensitivity/epsilon relation with
OpenDP, and invokes the measurement. Ordinary R random numbers are never used
for fitting noise. If OpenDP is missing or rejects the relation, fitting fails
closed.

OpenDP exposes the constructor behind its `contrib` feature; the recorded
backend version and feature status require independent review. The proof
assumes finite clipped inputs, correct L1 sensitivity and public-input claims,
correct OpenDP implementation/privacy map, complete basic composition, and no
confidential side channel. OpenDP's exact-rational handling of float inputs and
internal discrete sampler avoid a hand-written idealized continuous sampler.

The bounded aggregate exists transiently inside restricted R memory before the
OpenDP measurement; it is never returned, logged, cached, or serialized. That
boundary requires mechanism-level security review. Every fit creates a ledger
entry with release ID, time, unit, adjacency, requested/realized epsilon and
delta, backend/version, every query/dimension, and a refit warning.

The noiseless `backend = "public"` is accepted only with
`public_source = TRUE` for fully public examples. It reports
`formal_dp = FALSE` and is never an alternative for confidential data.

# What is generated after the private release?

`generate_pmx()` is post-processing: it reads the released summaries and
public configuration, not source rows. It creates new IDs, event rows,
observation times, endpoint values, covariates, censoring states, and a restored
public schema. The privacy argument requires only that this stage consult no
confidential data; its statistical construction is documented separately in
`vignette("pmxSynthData-simulation-method")`.

# Post-processing and release status

Repeated generation, constraint repair, generated-only validation, and plots
based only on the model consume no new budget. The user seed controls only
post-processing; fitting has no user privacy-noise seed.

| Object | Release status | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| OpenDP fitted model and ledger | DP release, subject to governance | Accounted mechanism output |
| Data generated solely from it | Releasable post-processing | No source access |
| Generated-only validation | Releasable post-processing | Uses generated data only |
| More seeds from the same model | Releasable post-processing | No refit |
| `compare_pmx(source, mock)` | Restricted, not automatically releasable | New source summaries |
| Fit logs or unnoised aggregates | Never release | Outside the DP contract |
| Another fit | New composed DP release | Reads source again |

`compare_pmx()` marks each component. Empirical attacks can find bugs; the
tests include an intentionally broken mechanism detected by a simple audit.
Passing an audit is never a proof because rare events and finite samples can
hide a violation.

# Limitations and independent review

The prototype learns low-order bounded marginals: count, generalized regimen,
timing presence, endpoint grid means, covariate moments/categories, and
censoring summaries. It does not learn detailed covariance, covariate-response
relationships, rare regimens, raw residuals, detailed missingness, or a
high-dimensional joint distribution. Much generated variability is public and
broad. Mock data must not be interpreted scientifically.

The implementation has not received the required independent DP-specialist
review. Before production, reviewers must inventory all confidential access,
verify sensitivity/composition, audit serialization/logging, test the exact
OpenDP version/platform, assess side channels and OpenDP's exact-rational and
discrete internal sampling assumptions,
and complete privacy, legal, information-security, and governance review.

Final practical checklist:

- [ ] Unit is one complete subject; adjacency is add-or-remove.
- [ ] Epsilon is approved as an influence limit; delta is correctly justified.
- [ ] Every item labeled public was established independently of confidential
      values.
- [ ] Rows, doses, occasions, observations, cells, and numeric domains are
      defensibly bounded.
- [ ] Every query is in the ledger and composition stays within the request.
- [ ] OpenDP version, mechanism checks, and assumptions were independently
      reviewed.
- [ ] Serialized model has no IDs, rows, profiles, templates, residuals,
      unnoised aggregates, or confidential levels.
- [ ] New datasets reuse the existing model instead of refitting.
- [ ] Source comparisons remain restricted unless separately privatized.
- [ ] Generated timing, endpoints, infusions, censoring, schema, and domains
      pass validation.
- [ ] Formal privacy is described as bounded, not absolute, and not as legal
      anonymity, organizational authorization, or scientific fidelity.
